headline: Who Pays When the River Dies
context: Since 2019, at least 14 major river contamination events have disrupted water supply in Selangor, Johor, and Penang, affecting over 8 million people.

cards.big: Fourteen chemical contamination events. Zero companies paid full cleanup.
cards.sub: The public pays — through water bills, health costs, and supply disruptions.

cards.big: Environmental Quality Act 1974 maximum fine: RM500,000.
cards.sub: The 2020 Selangor contamination cost RM180M in emergency response alone.

cards.big: DOE has 340 enforcement officers for 48,000 registered facilities.
cards.sub: Inspection rate: once every 12 years per facility. Most violations detected after contamination.

cards.big: 46% of monitored rivers are Class III or worse.
cards.sub: Class III is unsuitable for body contact. These rivers supply drinking water treatment plants.

cards.big: Privatise the profit, socialise the poison.
cards.sub: The polluter-pays principle exists in law. In practice, the consumer pays through every tap.

cards.big: Raise fines to match actual damage. Fund DOE to inspect before contamination, not after. The current system subsidises pollution.
